card.intuition: Apply Bayes' theorem assuming features are conditionally independent given the class: P(y|x) ∝ P(y)·∏P(xⱼ|y). Gaussian NB models P(xⱼ|y) as N(μⱼᵧ, σ²ⱼᵧ). Despite the 'naive' independence assumption, it works surprisingly well for text and other sparse high-dimensional data.
card.analogy: Diagnosing a patient using a medical checklist: assume each symptom provides independent evidence for each disease. Multiply the probabilities for each observed symptom per disease and multiply by the disease prevalence — the disease with the highest score wins.
card.time_complexity: O(n·d) to train; O(k·d) to predict (k = number of classes)
when_to_use: Text classification (spam, sentiment) — Multinomial NB works well with bag-of-words
Fast training and prediction when features are approximately independent
Strong baseline especially when training data is small
gotchas: Zero probability for unseen feature-class combinations — use Laplace smoothing: add 1 to all counts
Log probabilities to avoid numerical underflow when multiplying many small probabilities
Gaussian NB assumes normally distributed continuous features — check this assumption

exercise.prompt: Implement Gaussian Naive Bayes: fit per-class mean/variance, predict using log-likelihoods.
hints: Log of Gaussian PDF: -0.5*log(2π*σ²) - (x-μ)²/(2σ²) — this avoids computing exp and handles numerical stability
exercise.test_code:
X_tr = [[1,2],[1.5,1.8],[2,1],[6,5],[7,6],[6.5,5.5]]
y_tr = [0,0,0,1,1,1]
gnb = GaussianNB().fit(X_tr, y_tr)
preds = gnb.predict(X_tr)
assert preds == y_tr
# Test on new points
assert gnb.predict([[1,1]])[0] == 0
assert gnb.predict([[7,5]])[0] == 1
print('PASS')
